cases.title: Lateral Movement — U4521@DOM2 to Domain Controller
cases.description: Anomalous Kerberos authentication chain detected: U4521@DOM2 authenticated from C102 to C4501, then C4501 to C892, then C892 to DC01 (domain controller). Pattern matches MITRE T1021 lateral movement via Remote Services.

cases.title: Suspicious PowerShell Execution Chain
cases.description: PowerShell process spawned by cmd.exe with encoded command argument on host C3847. Process tree shows unusual parent chain: svchost.exe → cmd.exe → powershell.exe with -EncodedCommand flag.

cases.title: Data Exfiltration via DNS Tunneling
cases.description: Abnormal DNS query volume from C1923: 2,400+ queries in 10 minutes to subdomain pattern matching base64-encoded data tunneling. Destination resolves to external IP outside corporate range.

cases.title: Credential Dumping — Mimikatz Detection
cases.description: Process execution of mimikatz.exe detected on C587. Binary hash matches known Mimikatz variant. Process accessed LSASS memory (pid 672). Credentials may have been extracted.

cases.title: Brute Force Authentication — Multiple Failures
cases.description: 1,842 failed NTLM authentication attempts from C9234 targeting 15 different user accounts over 2-hour window. Pattern consistent with password spraying. No successful authentications detected.

cases.title: Persistence — Scheduled Task Created
cases.description: New scheduled task 'WindowsUpdateCheck' created on C4102 by U7823@DOM2 executing payload from C:\Windows\Temp\svc.exe. Binary not in approved software inventory. Task set to run every 15 minutes.

cases.title: Network Scan — Internal Reconnaissance
cases.description: Host C2891 initiated connections to 3,200 unique internal IPs on port 445 (SMB) within 5 minutes. Pattern consistent with network enumeration for file shares. Source process: nmap.

cases.title: Privilege Escalation — Service Account Misuse
cases.description: Service account SYSTEM on C6721 used for interactive logon — normally only batch/service logon types. Subsequent process execution includes cmd.exe and whoami.exe, suggesting hands-on-keyboard activity.
